evidence_quotes.quote: demand that consumers destroy any copies of legally obtained software

paragraphs.text: As someone who has seen the rise of the internet, I think there is one particular suggestion I'd like to make. When I was younger, I could buy video games, have a CD, and have full ownership of the software. In recent years especially, there have been several statements and practices by large companies to curtail ownership of software (https://www.ign.com/articles/ubisoft-exec-says-gamers-need-to-get-comfortable-not-owning-their-games-for-subscriptions-to-take-off / https://www.gamedeveloper.com/business/-i-overwatch-1-i-will-permanently-shut-down-on-october-2-says-blizzard / https://lifehacker.com/entertainment/not-all-switch-2-cartridges-have-games-on-them and more), to the detriment of consumers. Consumers pay money for software, and it is often not clearly communicated when service will end, it is in fact unilaterally decided when a consumer can and cannot access software after a publisher or game company decides it is no longer going to support this software. If at all possible, please, look into this. As citizens of the European Union, we are being forced more and more to give up ownership of our products, and do not even have the option, even at an inflated price, to OWN them. Yet still, we are expected to put an increasing amount of money toward these titles, these pieces of art, only to have them taken away, and for all intents and purposes unilaterally destroyed. Ubisoft has even gone as far as to demand that consumers destroy any copies of legally obtained software, when they decide to cut off your access (https://www.ubisoft.com/legal/documents/eula/en-US as seen on 30/07/2025). The lack of ownership and permanence of video-games, as art, and as a consumer product, is something that needs to be addressed - sooner rather than later, because it is this very impermanence that allows for increased exploitation. Not only can they use dark patterns in their methodology, not only can they trick minors into spending large amounts of money, they can then also take everything purchased away - on a whim. This didn't always use to be the case, and it presents massive overreach by companies in addition to all the other detrimental practices. The near monopoly on certain platforms makes it even more predatory, and there is a lawsuit underway in the Netherlands, my home country, about this very problem (https://www.gamesindustry.biz/sony-faces-dutch-lawsuit-over-artificially-high-playstation-prices). So I plead - please, examine not only their practices inside of software, but the practices surrounding software that exacerbate the already problematic digital landscape concerning software and ownership.
